55	# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
56	# PRISMA-trAIce compliance checklist (abbreviated for machine checks)
57	# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
58	#
59	# PRISMA-trAIce is a documented extension of PRISMA 2020 for AI-
60	# generated systematic reviews. Reference: Cochrane AI Methods Group
61	# guidance, 2025. The full 26-item list requires manual attestation;
62	# below are the subset of items that can be verified machine-side.
63	
64	# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
65	# M-30 (2026-04-20): abbreviation-aware sentence boundary detection for PT11.
66	#
67	# V19 aborted on PT11 because the prior regex treated `vs.` as a sentence
68	# terminator. A well-cited sentence like
69	#   "diarrhea (10.7% vs. 4.8%), nausea (8.1% vs. 2.7%),
70	#    and vomiting (5.7% vs. 1.2%).[7]"
71	# was scored as 4 uncited decimals because the lookahead from 4.8, 8.1, 2.7,
72	# 5.7 stopped at the nearest "vs. " and never reached "[7]" at the real
73	# sentence end.
74	#
75	# The abbreviation list below is generalizable English orthography — NOT a
76	# clinical-domain hard-code. Every domain that uses standard English prose
77	# (policy, materials, energy, due-diligence) benefits from the same list.
78	# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
79	
80	# M-30 pass-3 (addressing Codex pass-2 blocker): `etc.`, `U.S.`,
81	# `et al.` and other multi-segment acronyms also have sentence-final
82	# vs mid-sentence ambiguity. Previous pass-2 design treated them as
83	# ALWAYS non-boundary, which let `4.2%, 5.3%, 6.4%, 7.5% in the U.S. A
84	# separate claim.[1]` falsely pass PT11. Pass-3 restricts
85	# ALWAYS_NONBOUNDARY to only tokens that are virtually never
86	# sentence-final in practice; everything else routes through
87	# context-dependent next-char disambiguation.
88	#
89	# ALWAYS_NONBOUNDARY (virtually never ends a sentence):
90	#   - comparatives `vs.`, `v.`
91	#   - Latin connectives `cf.`, `viz.`
92	#   - titles `Dr.`, `Mr.`, `Mrs.`, `Ms.`, `Prof.`, `Sr.`, `Jr.`, `Rev.`
93	#     (practically always followed by a proper-noun name; the very
94	#     rare "She is a Dr." sentence-final usage is an accepted
95	#     false-negative since decimals don't usually precede titles).
96	#
97	# CONTEXT_DEPENDENT (resolved by next non-whitespace char — digit/
98	# paren/lowercase → non-boundary; uppercase/EOL → boundary):
99	#   - end-of-list `etc`
100	#   - document references (Fig, Ref, No, pp, Vol, Ch, Sec, App, ...)
101	#   - organisations (Inc, Ltd, Co, Corp, Gov, Dept)
102	#   - months (Jan-Dec)
103	#   - multi-segment acronyms (e.g, i.e, U.S, U.K, E.U) — routed via
104	#     the multi-segment detector in `_is_abbreviation_period`.
105	#   - `et al` — routed via the special-case detector.
106	_PT11_ALWAYS_NONBOUNDARY = frozenset([
107	    # Comparatives — never end a sentence
108	    "vs", "v",
109	    # Latin connectives that always introduce content after them
110	    "cf", "viz",
111	    # Titles — followed by proper-noun, treated as non-boundary.
112	    # Edge case: "She is a Dr." sentence-final; accepted false-negative
113	    # (decimals rarely precede sentence-final titles in research prose).
114	    "Dr", "Mr", "Mrs", "Ms", "Prof", "Sr", "Jr", "Rev",
115	])
116	
117	_PT11_CONTEXT_DEPENDENT = frozenset([
118	    # End-of-list marker — can be sentence-final ("items, etc. We then...")
119	    # or mid-sentence ("items, etc. and other things")
120	    "etc",
121	    # Document references — usually followed by a number
122	    "Fig", "Figs", "Ref", "Refs", "Eq", "Eqs",
123	    "No", "Nos", "pp", "Vol", "Ch", "Sec", "App",
124	    # Organisations — can be sentence-final ("Eli Lilly Inc. We...") or
125	    # mid-sentence ("Eli Lilly Inc. reported")
126	    "Inc", "Ltd", "Co", "Corp", "Gov", "Dept",
127	    # Months — can be sentence-final ("in Jan. We...") or followed by
128	    # a day number ("Jan. 15, 2020")
129	    "Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "Jun", "Jul", "Aug",
130	    "Sep", "Sept", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec",
131	])
132	
133	
134	# M-30 pass-5 (addressing Codex pass-4 blocker): ALL-CAPS after a
135	# context-dependent abbreviation can be either an acronym chain
136	# ("U.S. FDA database", mid-sentence) or a new-sentence subject
137	# ("U.S. FDA approved the summary", boundary). Disambiguate by
138	# looking at the WORD AFTER the acronym:
139	#   - If next-next word is a verb form (modal/auxiliary or -ed/-ing
140	#     past-tense/gerund) → new sentence (boundary).
141	#   - Otherwise → acronym chain continuation (non-boundary).
142	_PT11_VERB_INDICATORS = frozenset([
143	    # BE forms
144	    "is", "was", "are", "were", "be", "been", "being",
145	    # HAVE forms
146	    "has", "had", "have", "having",
147	    # DO forms
148	    "does", "did", "do", "done", "doing",
149	    # Modal auxiliaries
150	    "can", "may", "might", "will", "would",
151	    "could", "should", "must", "shall", "ought",
152	    # Common present-tense reporting verbs (-s suffix) that frequently
153	    # follow an acronym subject in research prose
154	    "reports", "issues", "approves", "states", "notes",
155	    "finds", "observes", "concludes", "recommends", "announces",
156	    "demonstrates", "publishes", "releases", "confirms",
157	    "establishes", "shows", "suggests",
158	])
159	
160	
161	# Title-case words that STRONGLY signal a sentence boundary when they
162	# follow a context-dependent abbreviation (Codex pass-3 addition).
163	# This list lets the boundary rule correctly split cases like
164	# "U.S. The trial..." (boundary) while keeping "U.S. FDA database..."
165	# (mid-sentence continuation, an all-caps acronym follows an acronym).
166	# Generalizable English discourse markers — no domain-specific terms.
167	_PT11_SENTENCE_STARTER_WORDS = frozenset([
168	    # Articles and demonstratives
169	    "The", "This", "That", "These", "Those", "An",
170	    # Pronouns that commonly open sentences
171	    "He", "She", "It", "They", "We", "You",
172	    # Discourse markers / transitional adverbs
173	    "However", "Moreover", "Furthermore", "Thus", "Therefore",
174	    "Nonetheless", "Nevertheless", "Conversely", "Meanwhile",
175	    "Subsequently", "Previously", "Historically", "Recently",
176	    "Currently", "Ultimately", "Overall", "Finally",
177	    "Similarly", "Alternatively", "Separately", "Additionally",
178	    "Notably", "Importantly", "Specifically", "Generally",
179	    "Indeed", "Also",
180	    # Conjunctions at sentence start
181	    "And", "But", "Or",
182	    # Prepositional / conditional openers
183	    "In", "On", "At", "When", "While", "Where",
184	    "If", "Though", "Although", "After", "Before", "During",
185	    # Sequential openers
186	    "First", "Second", "Third", "Next",
187	])
188	
189	
190	def _is_abbreviation_period(text: str, period_pos: int) -> bool:
191	    """True if text[period_pos] == '.' and the period is an abbreviation
192	    terminator (NOT a sentence boundary).
193	
194	    Disambiguation:
195	      1. ALWAYS_NONBOUNDARY tokens (vs, cf, viz, titles) are
196	         non-boundary unconditionally.
197	      2. CONTEXT_DEPENDENT tokens (etc, months, orgs, doc-refs),
198	         multi-segment acronyms (e.g, i.e, U.S, U.K, E.U), and the
199	         `et al.` special-case all resolve by the next
200	         non-whitespace character:
201	           - digit / `(` / `-` / `+` → non-boundary
202	           - lowercase letter        → non-boundary
203	           - uppercase letter        → boundary (new sentence starts)
204	           - end-of-string           → boundary
205	    """
206	    if period_pos < 0 or period_pos >= len(text) or text[period_pos] != ".":
207	        return False
208	    # Walk back over alphabetic characters + embedded periods to find
209	    # the token that ends at period_pos.
210	    start = period_pos - 1
211	    while start >= 0 and (text[start].isalpha() or text[start] == "."):
212	        start -= 1
213	    token = text[start + 1:period_pos]
214	    if not token:
215	        return False
216	
217	    # Bucket 1: always non-boundary (short-circuit).
218	    if token in _PT11_ALWAYS_NONBOUNDARY:
219	        return True
220	
221	    # Determine whether this is a context-dependent abbreviation:
222	    # explicit list, multi-segment acronym, or "et al.".
223	    is_context_dependent = token in _PT11_CONTEXT_DEPENDENT
224	
225	    if not is_context_dependent and "." in token:
226	        parts = [p for p in token.split(".") if p]
227	        if parts and all(len(p) <= 2 and p.isalpha() for p in parts):
228	            is_context_dependent = True  # e.g, i.e, U.S, U.K, E.U
229	
230	    if not is_context_dependent and token == "al":
231	        pre_start = max(0, start + 1 - 3)
232	        if text[pre_start:start + 1].endswith("et "):
233	            is_context_dependent = True  # "et al."
234	
235	    if not is_context_dependent:
236	        return False
237	
238	    # Resolve by the next non-whitespace character AND the shape of
239	    # the next word.
240	    after = period_pos + 1
241	    while after < len(text) and text[after] in " \t":
242	        after += 1
243	    if after >= len(text):
244	        return False  # end-of-input → real boundary
245	    nxt = text[after]
246	    if nxt.isdigit() or nxt in "(-+":
247	        return True  # "Fig. 3", "Jan. 15", "U.S. 2023 report"
248	    if nxt.islower():
249	        return True  # "Inc. reported", "etc. and more", "U.S. market"
250	
251	    # Uppercase next char — inspect the whole next word.
252	    word_end = after
253	    while word_end < len(text) and (text[word_end].isalpha() or text[word_end] in "-'"):
254	        word_end += 1
255	    next_word = text[after:word_end]
256	    alpha_only = "".join(c for c in next_word if c.isalpha())
257	
258	    # 1-char uppercase word: article "A" or pronoun "I" at sentence start.
259	    if len(alpha_only) == 1:
260	        return False
261	
262	    # Capture the word immediately after `next_word` (same-structure
263	    # lookahead — used to disambiguate both ALL-CAPS acronym chains and
264	    # Title-case proper nouns).
265	    nnext_start = word_end
266	    while nnext_start < len(text) and text[nnext_start] in " \t":
267	        nnext_start += 1
268	    nnext_end = nnext_start
269	    while nnext_end < len(text) and (text[nnext_end].isalpha() or text[nnext_end] in "-'"):
270	        nnext_end += 1
271	    nnext_word = text[nnext_start:nnext_end]
272	    nnext_lc = nnext_word.lower()
273	
274	    # ALL-CAPS multi-char word — usually an acronym. Distinguish by
275	    # looking at the next-next word:
276	    #   "U.S. FDA database"  → database is not a verb  → non-boundary
277	    #   "U.S. FDA approved"  → approved ends in -ed    → boundary
278	    #   "U.S. CDC reported"  → reported ends in -ed    → boundary
279	    #   "U.S. EPA reports"   → reports in verb list    → boundary
280	    #   "U.S. FDA is"        → is is a modal           → boundary
281	    if alpha_only.isupper():
282	        if _looks_like_verb_form(nnext_lc):
283	            return False  # ACRONYM + verb → new sentence
284	        return True  # ACRONYM + non-verb → acronym chain continuation
285	
286	    # Explicit sentence-starter discourse markers and articles →
287	    # sentence boundary. Catches "U.S. The trial...",
288	    # "Inc. Separately reports...", "et al. However it was...".
289	    if next_word in _PT11_SENTENCE_STARTER_WORDS:
290	        return False
291	
292	    # Title-case word (not in starter list) — likely a proper noun.
293	    # Further disambiguate by looking at the next-next word. If that
294	    # word starts with a lowercase letter, the Title-case word is
295	    # probably part of an ongoing noun phrase ("U.K. Biobank data",
296	    # "U.S. Pfizer reported"). If the next-next word is uppercase or
297	    # absent, default to boundary (safer).
298	    if nnext_start < len(text):
299	        nn = text[nnext_start]
300	        if nn.islower():
301	            return True  # proper-noun continuation — mid-sentence
302	    # No lowercase continuation → treat as sentence boundary.
303	    return False
304	
305	
306	def _looks_like_verb_form(word_lowercase: str) -> bool:
307	    """Heuristic verb-form detector on a lowercased word. True if
308	    the word is a common modal/auxiliary, a -ed past-tense form, or
309	    an -ing gerund/participle. Accepts occasional noun false-positives
310	    (e.g. "offering", "meeting") since the consequence is a PT11
311	    false-FAIL — recoverable by re-running — while the alternative
312	    is a false-PASS that lets fabrication through the audit gate."""
313	    if not word_lowercase:
314	        return False
315	    if word_lowercase in _PT11_VERB_INDICATORS:
316	        return True
317	    if len(word_lowercase) >= 4 and word_lowercase.endswith("ed"):
318	        return True
319	    if len(word_lowercase) >= 5 and word_lowercase.endswith("ing"):
320	        return True
321	    return False
322	
323	
324	# Sentence-terminator regex. Matches `.`, `!`, `?` when followed by
325	# whitespace, end-of-string, or an inline citation `[N]` / `[#ev:...]`
326	# (reports commonly write `sentence.[7] Next sentence...`).
327	_SENTENCE_END_REGEX = re.compile(r"[.!?](?=\s|$|\[)")
328	
329	
330	def _skip_trailing_citation_brackets(text: str, pos: int) -> int:
331	    """Advance `pos` past any `[...]` citation brackets that immediately
332	    follow. So `sentence.[7][#ev:x]` with `pos` at the `.` advances past
333	    both brackets. Returns the position after the last closing `]`.
334	
335	    This is needed because PT11 treats `.[7]` as one sentence-end unit:
336	    the `[7]` cites the PRIOR sentence, so it should be part of that
337	    sentence's "end" — not the start of the next sentence.
338	    """
339	    while pos < len(text) and text[pos] == "[":
340	        close = text.find("]", pos)
341	        if close < 0:
342	            break
343	        pos = close + 1
344	    return pos
345	
346	
347	def _next_real_sentence_end(text: str) -> int | None:
348	    """Return the position just past the first real sentence terminator
349	    in `text`, INCLUDING any trailing `[N]` / `[#ev:...]` citations. So
350	    for `sentence.[7] Next` the return value points at the space after
351	    `]`, letting lookahead snippets include the citation.
352	
353	    Returns None if no real terminator is found. Skips
354	    abbreviation-period false positives."""
355	    for m in _SENTENCE_END_REGEX.finditer(text):
356	        period_pos = m.start()
357	        if text[period_pos] == "." and _is_abbreviation_period(text, period_pos):
358	            continue
359	        return _skip_trailing_citation_brackets(text, period_pos + 1)
360	    return None
361	
362	
363	def _prev_real_sentence_end(text: str) -> int:
364	    """Return the index of the last char of the last real sentence
365	    (the terminator char or, if the terminator is followed by citation
366	    brackets, the closing `]` of the last trailing bracket).
367	
368	    So for `First sentence.[1] Current claim`, the return value points
369	    at the `]` of `[1]` — caller's `back_text[last_end+1:]` then starts
370	    cleanly in the new sentence and does NOT see `[1]`, which correctly
371	    belongs to the prior sentence.
372	
373	    Returns -1 if no terminator is found. Skips abbreviation-period
374	    false positives."""
375	    last = -1
376	    for m in _SENTENCE_END_REGEX.finditer(text):
377	        period_pos = m.start()
378	        if text[period_pos] == "." and _is_abbreviation_period(text, period_pos):
379	            continue
380	        # Advance past trailing citation brackets so they end this sentence.
381	        end_pos = _skip_trailing_citation_brackets(text, period_pos + 1)
382	        # `end_pos` is the position AFTER the last `]` (or after the `.`
383	        # when no brackets follow). Return that position minus 1 so the
384	        # caller's `back_text[last + 1:]` starts on the next char.
385	        last = end_pos - 1
386	    return last
387

451	    def to_json_dict(self) -> dict[str, Any]:
452	        d = asdict(self)
453	        # I-deepfix-001 B4-render #16 (#1344): keep byte-identity for a run with NO waived rule —
454	        # emit the ``waived`` key ONLY on the rule(s) that positively carry a waiver (a same-family
455	        # PT03 override). A rule at the inert default (waived=False) drops the key entirely, so the
456	        # legacy evaluator_rule_checks.json (which never had this key) is byte-for-byte unchanged.
457	        for _rc in d.get("rule_checks", []) or []:
458	            if isinstance(_rc, dict) and not _rc.get("waived"):
459	                _rc.pop("waived", None)
460	        return d

464	        return sum(1 for r in self.rule_checks if r.passed)

468	        return sum(1 for r in self.rule_checks if not r.passed)
